quotes.text: The victory poem of Deborah and Barak, aside from dramatic details about the death of the enemy, is chiefly a song of thanks and praise to the Lord. Some stanzas also extol valor in the armies and the preeminence of Deborah (Judg. 5:7, 12, 15). The bold courage of the Kenite woman, Jael, is praised, in contrast to the poignant bereavement of the mother of Sisera (Judg. 5:24-30). Literarily, Judges 5 is highly reputed as poetry. Paired reiterations tell the death of Sisera (Judg. 5:27). Remembering what the function of the priesthood should have been (Num. 27:21), we conclude that the priests were either failing or ignored as spiritual advisers; a good woman, Deborah, became the messenger of the Lord to Israel. The name Deborah (meaning "bee") and its diminutive forms (e.g., Debbie) have been popular in many cultures since hertime.
quotes.speaker: Ellis T. Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, p.215

quotes.text: A line-by-line count of syllables in a poem will often disclose that it was the overall length of line or strophe, and not the recurring accent pattern, that was most important to the poet. [The Song of Deborah has nine parts.1) 5:1-9 2) 5:10-13 3) 5:14-16 4) 5:17-20 5) 5:21-22 6) 5:23 7) 5:24-27 8) 5:28-30 9) 5:31]
quotes.speaker: Robert G. Boling
quotes.attr: Robert G. Boling, Judges: The Anchor Bible, 106

quotes.text: John calls this place "Armageddon." Just what he had in mind is unclear due to ambiguity with the term. The Greek form is Harmagdn. John states that this is a transliteration of a Hebrew word, but he does not supply the word. Many feel it should be "har-magedon," meaning "the mount of Megiddo." If this is the case then the reference would be to an ancient fortress that guarded one of the major highways going through the Jezreel valley to and from Jerusalem. Megiddo was the site for a number of famous battles, perhaps the most celebrated being the defeat of Sisera and his army when "the stars in their courses fought against Sisera" (Judg. 5:19-20). Also in the valley Esdraelon (Jezreel), Gideon's three hundred soldiers roundly defeated the Midianites (see Judg. 7); Saul and Jonathan met their end (see 1 Sam. 31:1-6); and king Josiah fought Pharaoh Neco and was killed (see 2 Kgs. 23:29-30).
quotes.speaker: Richard D. Draper
quotes.source: Opening the Seven Seals: The Visions of John the Revelator
quotes.attr: Richard D. Draper, Opening the Seven Seals: The Visions of John the Revelator, p.179

quotes.text: Armageddon. A Greek transliteration from the Hebrew Har Megiddon, or Mountain of Megiddo. The valley of Megiddo is in the western portion of the plain of Esdraelon 50 miles north of Jerusalem. Several times the valley of Megiddo was the scene of violent and crucial battles during [Old Testament] times (Judg. 5:19; 2 Kgs. 9:27; 23:29). A great and final conflict taking place at the second coming of the Lord is called the battle of Armageddon because it too will be fought in the same locale and will be decisive. See Zech. 11-14, especially 12:11; Rev. 16:14-21.
quotes.speaker: Hoyt W. Brewster
quotes.source: I Come Quickly: The Last Days and Beyond
quotes.attr: Hoyt W. Brewster, Jr., Behold, I Come Quickly: The Last Days and Beyond, p.95-96

quotes.text: The River Kishon flows in a northwest direction through the Jezreel Valley until it empties into the Mediterranean Sea near present-day Haifa. Because the land is quite flat, the river is usually not much more than a sluggish stream. In times of unusually hard rains, however, it may overflow its banks and flood the surrounding land, making it marshy and nearly impassable. The song of Deborah seems to suggest that just such an unexpected downpour, accompanied by thunder and lightning, suddenly struck the area. The chariots of Sisera bogged down in the resulting overflow of the Kishon River, making it possible for the smaller forces of Deborah and Barak to achieve victory. Deborah rightly saw in this event the hand of the Lord and gave him credit for the victory (see vs. 30).
quotes.attr: Old Testament Student Manual: Genesis-2 Samuel, 254

notes: Gideon (Judges 6:1-8:32) a beginning of oppression by Midianites (6:1-10) o negative chronological note b Gideon's divine call; his destruction of idolatry at Ophrah (6:11-40) o Gideon's family's good involvement o Gideon's fleece laid on the ground to collect dew (which encourages Gideon to lead Israel to victory) o Gideon pleases Jehovah, opposes the people o good results: Jehovah in control c troops gather for the battle (7:1-14) o army is made smaller, "that Israel may not boast" o intertribal cooperation assumed o good results: Jehovah in control d TURNING POINT: Jehovah gives victory (7:15-22)* c' troops disperse after the battle (7:23-8:21) o the pursuit; attempt to enlarge the army; boasting o intertribal bickering : o tragic results: Jehovah not in control b' Gideon's call by Israelites and his lapse into idolatry at Ophrah (8:22-27) o Gideon's family's bad involvement o Gideon's garment spread out (on the ground) to collect spoils (which leads to idolatry) o Gideon cooperates with the people, displeases Jehovah o tragic results: Jehovah not in control o ' a' end of oppression by Midianites (8:28-32) o positive chronological note adapted from David A. Dorsey, The Literary Structure of the Old Testament
